steps.text: Direct questions about this policy to the CDC Operational Policy Management Team at operationalpolicy@cdc.gov.
Direct questions regarding appropriate collaborative activities to the Office of the General Counsel (OGC), CDC Branch, Department of Health & Human Services (HHS).
Refer questions and issues relating to gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government to the Human Resources Office (HRO), CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity.

preconditions.text: If there are questions about this policy
If there are questions regarding appropriate collaborative activities
If there are questions and issues relating to gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government

constraints.text: Gifts from foreign governments or international organizations, if authorized by the agency, are subject to the Foreign Gifts and Decorations Act (5 U.S.C. Section 7342).
Gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government are subject to the government-wide ethics regulations.

steps.text: Deposit the accepted money into the general fund of the Treasury as soon as practicable.

preconditions.text: An official or agent of the Government receives money for the Government from an outside source, and the funds do not constitute an authorized repayment, and the agency lacks specific statutory authority to retain the funds.

constraints.text: An agency may not augment its appropriation from an outside source without specific statutory authority to do so.

preconditions.text: For gifts supporting activities of individual employees, the principal beneficiary must be CDC rather than the employee, and the gift must be given in such a form that the money can be used even if the employee leaves CDC.

constraints.text: Conditional gifts do not include 'fee-for-service' transactions in which CDC is providing goods or services for the benefit of the donating party above and beyond what is normally available to the general public.

steps.text: Transfer any income from the lease or hire of accepted property to OFR for deposit with the Secretary of the Treasury.
Transfer proceeds from the liquidation of personal property to OFR for deposit with the Secretary of the Treasury.

preconditions.text: Income is received from the lease or hire of accepted property.
Proceeds are received from the liquidation of personal property.

constraints.text: Do not accept real property as a non-monetary gift.
Do not accept non-monetary gifts if the total costs to CDC associated with acceptance are expected to exceed the cost of purchasing a similar item and the cost of normal care and maintenance.
The donor is responsible for any valuation of gifts.
Accept the valuation the donor places on a tendered gift, unless the value is clearly unreasonable.
Transfer must occur within 24 hours of receipt.

Funds received by or for the United States Government or its agencies, including gifts to the government, are to be deposited in, and administered through, United States Government accounts and may not be deposited with or otherwise administered by other persons or organizations.

An outside organization (this includes a foundation whose mission is solely or in part to support CDC activities) may not serve as a financial intermediary for a third party in the donation of funds, equipment, supplies, or other resources.

steps.text: Begin conflict of interest review within the CIO.
Conduct a separate, independent conflict of interest review by the CDC Foundation.
Submit the gift to the CDC Conflict of Interest Review Panel for review.
Make an entry in the accounts for a gift from a will or trust.
Perform a balancing test to determine whether the benefits outweigh the risk that accepting the gift would reflect unfavorably upon CDC.

preconditions.text: The gift is from the CDC Foundation.
The gift is a monetary gift over $500.
The estate is settled.

constraints.text: The gift should not be accepted if any employee’s ability to carry out his or her responsibilities or official duties in a fair and objective manner is influenced.
The gift should not be accepted if acceptance of the gift could compromise the integrity of a government program or any official involved in that program.
The gift should not be accepted if the donor has an expectation of receiving a future benefit such as a contract award.
CDC may not accept a gift if it is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be met from the gift itself or from the income of the gift.
Neither the gift nor any conditions associated with the gift should exert undue influence over CDC programs and priorities.

steps.text: Complete the Appendix 2: Gift Acceptance Validity Survey for additional scrutiny.
Conduct additional scrutiny to determine if the company should be excluded as a gift source.
Scrutinize the proposed gift using Appendix 2: the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey.
Complete the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey to determine whether the gift is acceptable to the CIO.
Review recommendations regarding excluded sources made by the Advisory Committee to the Director in its May 2016 report in collaboration with OGC.

preconditions.text: A company has competed or is competing for a contract with CDC, and offers a monetary or non-monetary gift to CDC directly or through the CDC Foundation for a related or unrelated project.
A company has a negative public image (e.g., scrutinized in the media for questionable employee health practices) but is not technically a prohibited source.
A company had a contract with CDC to develop a surveillance system and now wants to contribute funds to a communications campaign through the CDC Foundation for the same priority issue.
A prohibited source offers a gift.
There is any question that a potential donor may be an excluded source.

constraints.text: The CIO gift review official must be especially aware of the increased probability that an actual or apparent conflict of interest may exist.

steps.text: Contact OFR, Office of Debt Management for disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to the agency.
Use first-class mail for the limited purpose of forwarding reimbursements to donors.
Contact the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity for guidance regarding disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to individual employees.
Return tangible items to the donor.
Pay the donor market value (retail cost of an item or service of like quality).
Destroy, share within the office, or donate items.

preconditions.text: If a gift cannot be accepted
Subject to OGC approval as a legally permissible use of appropriated funds
Subject to approval of a supervisor or organization head

constraints.text: Written notification must accompany all gift returns.

steps.text: Advise donors from commercial organizations that the acceptance of a gift does not constitute an implied or direct endorsement.

constraints.text: CDC will not accept a gift that is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be covered by the gift itself or from income earned from the gift, unless an Act of Congress has approved such expenditure.
Offers of property will not be accepted if the total costs associated with acceptance are expected to exceed the cost of purchasing a similar item and the cost of normal care and maintenance.
Gifts may not be accepted if the conditions placed on the gift create a conflict of interest.
Gifts tendered in order to secure an implied or direct endorsement are not accepted.
Gifts may not be accepted when the donor expresses, directly or indirectly, an expectation of receiving a future benefit, such as a contract award from CDC.

steps.text: Ensure the entertainment expenditure meets the necessary expense test.
Obtain pre-approval from OFR for gift funds expenditures for entertainment.
Pay for entertainment functions without a legitimate official purpose (such as an office holiday party) using personal funds.
Apply the same regulations and procedures in effect for travel supported by appropriated funds when using gift funds for official travel.
Consult with the OFR or the Ethics and Compliance Activity Office for assistance with sponsored travel.

preconditions.text: The entertainment furthers a valid, authorized function of CDC.
The function could not be otherwise accomplished from the government’s viewpoint without the expenditure.
The expenditure does not violate any restrictions imposed by the donor on the use of the funds.

constraints.text: Donated funds may not be used for entertainment that does not bear a legitimate official purpose.
Once received by CDC, gift funds become public funds and should be spent in the same manner as appropriated funds.

steps.text: Determine whether unsolicited grants or other types of awards made to CDC employees are intended for CDC or for the employee serving in an individual capacity.
Accept the funds as a gift and deposit them into the appropriate CDC gift fund account.
Seek guidance from the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity to avoid the potential for conflict of interest or other impropriety in the acceptance of funds from outside sources.
Seek guidance from OGC to ensure that conditions set out in grant award documents and agreements are legally appropriate.

preconditions.text: If unsolicited grants or other types of awards are made to CDC employees for scientific or other professional purposes
If an employee receives funds from any outside organization that supports the costs of CDC activities

constraints.text: All statutory requirements regarding the acceptance of conditional gifts must be met.
Grant funds must be used to advance CDC’s mission, and there must be no legal objections to CDC’s acceptance of these grants.
Such a gift should not be a gift to the employee.

steps.text: Become familiar with this policy and follow the processes provided in this document to respond to a gift made to CDC.
Contact the Office of the General Counsel, the Office of Financial Resources, or the CIO CDC Foundation Liaison.
Direct questions regarding user fees, collaborations, co-sponsorships, and the applicability of this policy in general to a given activity or gift to OGC.
Contact ECA regarding gifts to individual employees that are not covered by this policy, including all gifts from within or outside of the Federal government.
Contact the Ethics and Compliance Activity at ethics@cdc.gov.

preconditions.text: If CDC employees have questions regarding the acceptance of gifts as an individual and not to or on behalf of the agency.

constraints.text: ECA does not provide advice and guidance on agency ethics issues; such questions should be directed to OGC.

steps.text: Forward cash or cash equivalents for deposit to the Deputy Chief Financial Officer within 24 hours of receipt, accompanied by a brief memorandum stating the gift is pre-decisional and any accompanying documentation.
Mail gift fund checks to the designated USPS or Private Carrier Delivery address.
Transmit wired funds via pay.gov.
Deposit the gift in a suspense account.
Return monetary gift checks more than 90 days old to the recipient organization for return to the donor for reissuance.
Determine if the proposed gift has been recommended for approval by the Conflict-of-Interest panel, decide whether to accept the gift, and notify the donor of the determination.

preconditions.text: The gift is not officially accepted within the 24-hour period.
Monetary gift check is more than 90 days old.

constraints.text: In accordance with the Cash Management Policy, CDC-FM-2009-02, all offices must forward cash or cash equivalents within 24 hours of receipt.
For monetary gifts valued at not more than $2 million and unconditional gifts of personal property valued at not more than $5,000, authority rests with the CIO director.
For monetary gifts of more than $2 million, authority rests with the CDC COO.
The timeframe may be extended in cases where the decision on the acceptance of a gift is difficult (e.g., negotiation with the donor is required).

steps.text: Draft the letter of acceptance stating only that the monetary gift is being deposited into the 'component's gift fund account' and the purpose.
OFR deposits the money into the gift fund account.
Record the deposit as 'conditional' or 'unconditional' and code with the appropriate program segment code.
Promptly return any uncashed checks to the donor.
OFR draws a refund check from the Department of the Treasury.
Thank the donor and inform them that the donation will be considered for acceptance and they will be advised of a decision later.
CIO property custodians notify the Logistics Management Branch, OSSAM, of proposals for personal property gifts.
CIO property custodian submits a copy of the document accepting the gift to OSSAM.

preconditions.text: If the monetary gift is accepted
If the monetary gift is not accepted, or the donor refuses to accept CDC terms
If the donor's check has already been deposited
For non-monetary gifts
Upon receipt of the personal property gift

constraints.text: Do not refer to specific funds like 'Breast Cancer Gift Fund' or 'Emphysema Gift Fund' in the acceptance letter.

The notification must include: a description of the item (including manufacturer, model number, estimated value), the accountable area (custodial location code), and the purpose specified by the donor.

steps.text: Create a basic record in the on-line property system including estimated value, manufacturer's serial number, month/year received, physical location, CDC decal number, and purpose specified by the donor.
Determine at least annually if there will be excess funds not used within a 12-month period.
Request in writing that the Chief Financial Officer invest the excess funds in interest-bearing obligations of the United States or as otherwise authorized by law.
Provide CIOs with quarterly status sheets of all invested funds, including amounts, dates of investment, and maturity.
Return the monies to the same gift fund account to be used for the benefit of the CIOs.

preconditions.text: Upon notification of acceptance of the gift by the CIO property custodian.
It is determined that there are excess funds and the amount of these funds is $50,000 or more.
Upon liquidation of investments.

constraints.text: The director's memorandum must include the amount of funds invested, the source of funds, recommendations regarding purchase, and information concerning when these investments will be redeemed.
If an investment is redeemed before the maturity date, there may be loss of principal.
The spend plan must be aligned with the investment of the securities and redemption of the securities.

steps.text: Obligate or expend gift funds.
Determine whether CDC's interest in accepting a gift outweighs concerns that accepting the gift reflects unfavorably upon the agency or employee, or compromises program integrity.
Complete the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey.

preconditions.text: Gift funds must be received.

constraints.text: All responses answered unfavorably should be given considerable scrutiny.
OFR controls gift funds in accordance with strict internal funds controls.

steps.text: Determine if there is sensitivity in any matter pending before CDC that would affect the interest of the donor.
Determine if there are any effects of accepting the gift on entities that are inside/outside the agency or component.
Consult the Office of the General Counsel to provide guidance on problematic issues about the acceptance of the gift.
Identify the practical impact of the gift within CDC or CIO.
Identify why the gift is offered to CDC or CIO.
